# 4. Results

## 4.1 Alfalfa hay yields drop sharply with exposure to temperatures over `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)`

The coefficients of Equation (2) are presented in Table 2. We estimate Equation (2) across six different growing season weather aggregation windows; our preferred `r objs$window_selection$chosen_months`-month window yields an R-squared of `r fmt_num(objs$summary$rsq, 3)`. It is important to note that interpreting the estimated coefficients of the degree day variables requires caution, as a change in a degree day implies changes in the entire temperature distribution. However, several significant observations hold regardless of the aggregation window. Increased exposure to beneficial heat (degree days between `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$lower)` and `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)`) is positively correlated with crop yields, whereas exposure to harmful heat (degree days above `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)`) is negatively correlated. Table 2 makes this robustness concrete for the harmful-heat term: the above-`r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)` coefficient stays negative and significant at the 1% level in all six windows, ranging only from `r fmt_num(objs$relation$DD3_window_max, 2)` to `r fmt_num(objs$relation$DD3_window_min, 2)` (×10³). The relationship between temperature and alfalfa yields, using the `r objs$window_selection$chosen_months`-month window (Figure 5), shows that beneficial exposure below `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$lower)` carries a positive, significant degree-day coefficient (`r fmt_sci(objs$relation$DD1$estimate)`). Conversely, exposure from `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$lower)` to `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)` is small and statistically insignificant (`r fmt_sci(objs$relation$DD2$estimate)`), while exposure above `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)` carries a significant negative coefficient (`r fmt_sci(objs$relation$DD3$estimate)`), indicating negative impacts from warming. Although the specific thresholds differ, the pattern of beneficial and detrimental temperature exposures observed in Figure 5 aligns with previous studies [@schlenker2009; @tack2015; @shew2020]. The exposure histogram beneath the response curve in Figure 5 explains why so steep a penalty still produces only modest national losses today: the overwhelming majority of growing-season degree-days fall within the beneficial `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$lower)`–`r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)` window (county mean `r fmt_num(objs$summary$dd2_mean, 2)` degree-days), with only a thin tail currently above `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)` (mean `r fmt_num(objs$summary$dd3_mean, 2)`), the very tail that warming shifts rightward. Regarding precipitation, the estimates in Table 2 indicate that it positively affects yields up to approximately `r fmt_num(objs$relation$ppt_turning_point, 1)` inches, after which the benefit declines. Given that the growing season mean annual precipitation is only `r fmt_num(objs$summary$ppt_mean, 2)` inches, yields are unlikely to decline due to excessive annual precipitation. However, periodic destructive rainfall could still lead to yield declines, resulting from physical crop damage rather than agronomic limitations.

The national piecewise response masks substantial regional heterogeneity. Figure 6 partitions the country into four agro-climatic clusters and re-estimates the thresholds and degree-day slopes within each. The clusters trace familiar geography: a vast `r objs$clusters$northern$name` belt across the northern tier and Midwest (`r fmt_num(objs$clusters$northern$acres, 0)` thousand alfalfa acres, the bulk of national area), a `r objs$clusters$southern$name` zone spanning the South, a `r objs$clusters$humid$name` band through the eastern interior, and a small but intensely productive `r objs$clusters$desert$name` in California, Arizona, and Nevada. What matters for warming is the harmful-heat slope, and it varies enormously across these zones: the `r objs$clusters$southern$name` cluster loses the most yield for each degree-day above its threshold (`r fmt_num(objs$clusters$southern$slope, 2)` ×10³), followed by the `r objs$clusters$humid$name` band (`r fmt_num(objs$clusters$humid$slope, 2)`) and the `r objs$clusters$northern$name` belt (`r fmt_num(objs$clusters$northern$slope, 2)`). The striking exception is the `r objs$clusters$desert$name`: despite by far the highest heat exposure of any cluster (`r fmt_num(objs$clusters$desert$dd3_mean, 0)` degree-days above `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)`, against `r fmt_num(objs$clusters$full$dd3_mean, 0)` nationally) and the highest yields of any cluster (`r fmt_num(objs$clusters$desert$yield, 2)` tons per acre, against `r fmt_num(objs$clusters$humid$yield, 2)`–`r fmt_num(objs$clusters$northern$yield, 2)` in the other zones), its estimated harmful slope is almost flat (`r fmt_num(objs$clusters$desert$slope, 2)`): an in-sample signature of heavily irrigated production that buffers heat stress, and a caveat we return to in the discussion. The estimated thresholds move with the same logic, the upper knot rising to `r fmt_degC(objs$clusters$southern$Tmax)` in the heat-adapted `r objs$clusters$southern$name` zone against `r fmt_degC(objs$clusters$northern$Tmax)` in the `r objs$clusters$northern$name` belt, and the within-county fit is tightest for the desert (R² `r fmt_num(objs$clusters$desert$rsq, 2)`) and near `r fmt_num(objs$clusters$southern$rsq, 2)`–`r fmt_num(objs$clusters$northern$rsq, 2)` elsewhere. These cluster-specific responses are what give the county-level warming impacts their spatial texture, concentrating the deepest losses on the heat-sensitive South while leaving the irrigated West comparatively insulated.

## 4.2 Impact of uniform warming on alfalfa hay yield

The dominance of beneficial (at or below `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)`) and detrimental (above `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)`) temperature exposures over one another will determine the overall impact of warming. To determine which dominates, the study predicts yield impacts following a +1°C uniform temperature change. Figure 7 shows that relative to the current NOAA recommended baseline climate period of 1991-2020, a 1°C warming scenario produces an average `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact$s10)` decrease in alfalfa hay yields in the US. Altering the warming intensity also shows that larger declines are expected at hotter scenarios, with alfalfa hay yields expected to decline by `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact$s05)`, `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact$s15)`, `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact$s20)`, `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact$s25)`, and `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact$s30)` for a +0.5, +1.5, +2.0, +2.5, and +3.0°C uniform temperature change. Panel (a) of Figure 7 also shows the delta-method confidence intervals widening steadily as warming intensifies (narrow and comfortably below zero at +0.5°C but spanning several percentage points by +3.0°C), so while the central estimates deepen monotonically, the uncertainty around them grows in step.

Our baseline climate period of 1991 to 2020 follows NOAA recommendations, but alternative periods may be more informative [@ipcc1994]. Earlier periods, such as 1961 to 1990, show more significant anthropogenic trends due to sulfate aerosols, especially in the eastern USA [@karl1996]. Ideally, the 19th century would be the best baseline when anthropogenic impacts were minimal. Evaluating the +1°C alfalfa yield impact with different baselines reveals significant variations. The 1981-2010 period showed the highest impact of `r fmt_pct(objs$baseline[["1981_2010"]])`, while earlier periods, such as 1961-1990 (`r fmt_pct(objs$baseline[["1961_1990"]])`), exhibit substantial negative impacts. The lowest impact is in 1971-2000 at `r fmt_pct(objs$baseline[["1971_2000"]])`. These findings highlight the importance of considering various historical periods to understand climatic influences on agricultural productivity fully.

Hay harvesting presents unique challenges due to its multiple cuttings throughout the year, unlike crops such as corn and soybeans, which have specific growing seasons. The period from seeding to the last cut varies widely by location, making it difficult to define a uniform growing season. Our preferred specification aggregates data only over the first `r objs$window_selection$chosen_months` months within each state-defined range. To evaluate the robustness of the +1°C alfalfa yield impact, we independently adjust the weather data accumulation window. Our analysis reveals that the yield impact of a +1°C temperature increase varies with the data window. A 10-month window shows `r fmt_pct(objs$window$m10)`, while a 5-month window yields `r fmt_pct(objs$window$m5)`. Other windows, such as 6 months (`r fmt_pct(objs$window$m6)`) and 7 months (`r fmt_pct(objs$window$m7)`, our preferred specification), show decreasing impacts. Finally, non-alfalfa hay types show a slightly higher impact of `r fmt_pct(objs$window$hay_other)` following a +1°C warming scenario, indicating that different types of hay respond differently to warming temperatures.

The predicted impacts of a +1°C climate change scenario on U.S. alfalfa hay yields vary significantly by region (See Figure 7). Counties in the Northeast generally show positive trends (`r objs$county_share$Northeast$n_pos` of `r objs$county_share$Northeast$n` counties), with those in Connecticut (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$CT$s10)`), Vermont (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$VT$s10)`), Rhode Island (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$RI$s10)`), Massachusetts (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$MA$s10)`), and Maine (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$ME$s10)`) experiencing the most significant increases, likely due to the cooler climate mitigating heat stress. The South, characterized by already higher temperatures, largely experiences negative impacts, with counties in Texas (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$TX$s10)`), Louisiana (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$LA$s10)`), Oklahoma (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$OK$s10)`), Mississippi (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$MS$s10)`), and Florida (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$FL$s10)`) observing the sharpest declines in yields, possibly exacerbated by drought conditions. The Midwest and West both present a mixed response, with `r objs$county_share$Midwest$pct_pos`% and `r objs$county_share$West$pct_pos`% of Midwestern and Western counties, respectively, showing positive trends. Midwestern counties in Minnesota (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$MN$s10)`), Wisconsin (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$WI$s10)`), and Michigan (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$MI$s10)`) see slight gains, possibly benefiting from warmer temperatures improving growing conditions, while those in Ohio (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$OH$s10)`), South Dakota (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$SD$s10)`), Iowa (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$IA$s10)`), Indiana (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$IN$s10)`), and Illinois (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$IL$s10)`) face notable decreases. In the West, outcomes are diverse; counties in Washington (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$WA$s10)`), Oregon (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$OR$s10)`), and Montana (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$MT$s10)`) see minor improvements, suggesting some areas may benefit from modest warming, whereas counties in Idaho (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$ID$s10)`), Utah (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$UT$s10)`), Colorado (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$CO$s10)`), and California (`r fmt_signed(objs$impact_state$CA$s10)`), located in arid regions, encounter declines.

Figure 7 also illustrates the geographical changes in alfalfa yields with temperature increases across various regions. The impact on yields varies significantly with increasing temperature gradients. For example, with only a 0.5°C increase, counties in states like Connecticut, Maine, and Massachusetts experience an increase in yields. In contrast, counties in southern states such as Alabama, Arizona, and Florida see a notable decrease, with Florida experiencing a yield drop of `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_state$FL$s05, 2)`. As the temperature increase reaches 1.5°C, counties in Arizona and Florida show dramatic decreases in yields of `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_state$AZ$s15, 2)` and `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_state$FL$s15, 2)`, respectively. The negative impact intensifies at a 3°C increase, particularly affecting states like Florida and Texas with county yield reductions averaging `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_state$FL$s30, 2)` and `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_state$TX$s30, 2)`, respectively. Interestingly, northern states such as Connecticut, Maine, and Massachusetts continue to see yield increases or less severe decreases up to a 2°C temperature increase, after which the impact becomes neutral or negative. By contrast, southern and central states experience accelerating declines as temperatures rise, with Missouri and Kansas notably impacted by `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_state$MO$s30, 2)` and `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_state$KS$s30, 2)` decreases at a 3°C increase, coinciding with these states being major cattle-producing areas. These estimates indicate broader regional vulnerabilities in livestock production as temperatures continue to rise.

## 4.3 Warming impacts on forage availability and cattle inventory

The dependence of cattle inventory on local alfalfa supply established in Section 2 means the yield losses above do not stop at the field. The same southern and southwestern counties that lose the most yield lose the most forage: Figure 8 maps the predicted county-level change in alfalfa availability, and its deep-red core (Texas, the desert Southwest, and Florida) intensifies scenario by scenario, from faint losses at +0.5°C to pervasive declines by +3.0°C. On average a +1°C increase lowers availability by `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_avail$s10)` (declines appear in `r objs$livestock$avail_pct_neg_s10`% of counties), deepening to `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_avail$s30)` under +3.0°C. Because cattle inventory tracks availability, these losses carry into the livestock sector (Figure 9): average cattle inventory falls by `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_cattle$s10)` under +1°C, with `r objs$livestock$cattle_pct_neg_s10`% of counties declining, and by `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_cattle$s30)` under +3.0°C. Two features of that map stand out. First, the cattle response is visibly milder than the yield and availability shocks driving it: an elasticity below one passes only part of each forage loss through to inventory, so the roughly `r 100 - objs$livestock$cattle_pct_neg_s10`% of counties that avoid declines read as pale rather than deep red. Second, the handful of counties that actually gain cluster in the cooler northern Plains and upper Midwest (Montana, the Dakotas, and pockets of the central Rockies), where modest warming eases cold limitation. The steepest reductions, however, concentrate in the same already-hot southern and arid-western counties that bear the largest yield losses, reinforcing that projected warming threatens to widen the mismatch between forage supply and feed demand.
